front: Work: the narrator recites Dante's 'Canto of Ulysses' to Jean (Pikolo) while fetching soup, comparing it to 'the blast of a trumpet, the voice of God'
back: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz (Primo Levi)
work: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz

front: Work: uses 'Lager' for the camp and 'Ka-Be' for the infirmary where nurses confiscate spoons from the ill
back: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz (Primo Levi)
work: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz

front: Work: the narrator is told 'there is no why here' after having an icicle confiscated
back: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz (Primo Levi)
work: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz

front: Work: opening poem addresses 'you who live safe in your warm houses'; commands 'carve them into your hearts'; consider a woman 'without hair and without name'
back: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz (Primo Levi)
work: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz

front: Work: a French girl tells the narrator 'bite your lip, little brother' after he is beaten in an electrical warehouse
back: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz (Primo Levi)
work: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz

front: Work: closes with a meditation paralleling the Jewish V'ahavta; bricklayer Lorenzo smuggled food to the narrator
back: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz (Primo Levi)
work: If This Is a Man / Survival in Auschwitz

front: Work: 'Cerium' -- the narrator steals iron-cerium from a factory to make cigarette lighters, buying two months of bread
Work: 'Vanadium' -- the narrator recognizes former colleague Dr. Muller from his spelling of 'naphthenate' in a letter about defective varnish
Work: 'Carbon' -- final story narrating the cyclical journey of a single carbon atom
Work: 'Silver' -- set on the fictional island of Desolation with Welsh place names like Holywell Cave and Mt. Snowdon
Work: 'Nitrogen' -- contains the line 'matter is matter, neither noble nor vile, infinitely transformable'
Work: coined the term 'Gray Zone'; places Chaim Rumkowski of Lodz in it; includes 'Letters from Germans' and 'The Memory of the Offense'
back: The Drowned and the Saved (Primo Levi)

front: Author: had difficulty finding a thesis advisor due to anti-Semitic laws; worked analyzing mine residues and researching diabetes
Novel: a watchmaker named Mendel fights alongside violinist Gedalah behind enemy lines in Russia
Poem: recounts the 'monotonous horror' of mud in Auschwitz
